name: City tour through Simancas and the mouth of the Pisuerga River — circular along the Duero path
name_original: City tour por Simancas y desembocadura del río Pisuerga — circular por la senda del Duero
region_intro: Hiking around Simancas offers diverse landscapes within the Castile and León region of Spain. The area is characterized by the extensive Senda del Duero, which follows the Duero River, and features verdant fields, pine forests, and vineyards. Trails often traverse the Castilian-Leonese plateau, providing varied terrain from riverbanks to gentle elevations. The region also includes sections of the Camino de Madrid and routes near the Pisuerga River mouth. Best hiking trails around Simancas The most popular hiking route is City tour through Simancas and the mouth of the Pisuerga River — circular along the Duero path, a 7.9 miles (12.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 15 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the Duero path and the confluence of the Pisuerga River. Another top favourite among local hikers is Canal in the Simancas pine forest loop from Simancas, a moderate 5.2 miles (8.4 km) path. This trail leads through a tranquil pine forest alongside a canal. Local hikers also love the Castillo de Simancas – View of the Cortados Cliffs loop from Simancas, a 6.3 miles (10.2 km) trail leading through historical landmarks and offering views of the Cortados Cliffs, often completed in about 2 hours 46 minutes. Hiking around Simancas is defined by river paths along the Duero, serene pine forests, and open vineyard landscapes. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and challenging routes suitable for various ability levels. The routes in Simancas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 1000 hikers have used komoot to explore Simancas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Simancas?
A: There are over 190 hiking routes in the Simancas area, offering a wide variety of experiences for all skill levels.

Q: What are the typical difficulty levels for hiking trails in Simancas?
A: The trails around Simancas primarily feature easy and moderate difficulty levels. You'll find approximately 84 easy routes and 104 moderate routes, with a few more challenging options available for experienced hikers.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Simancas?
A: Hiking in Simancas offers diverse landscapes, from river paths along the Duero and Pisuerga rivers to serene pine forests and open vineyard landscapes characteristic of the Rueda Wine Route. You'll also traverse parts of the Castilian-Leonese plateau.

Q: Are there many circular hiking routes in Simancas?
A: Yes, many routes around Simancas are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1084992806/city-tour-through-simancas-and-the-mouth-of-the-pisuerga-river-circular-along-the-duero-path" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;City tour through Simancas and the mouth of the Pisuerga River — circular along the Duero path&lt;/a&gt; is a popular loop, as is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1276569" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal in the Simancas pine forest loop from Simancas&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What historical landmarks or attractions can I see along the trails in Simancas?
A: You can explore several historical landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10462858" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Simancas – View of the Cortados Cliffs loop from Simancas&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5837382" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Simancas&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2814203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Simancas Stone Bridge&lt;/a&gt; is another notable point of interest often encountered on river-side walks.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy the most about hiking in Simancas?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 100 reviews. Hikers often praise the varied terrain, the peaceful river paths, and the well-maintained trails through pine forests and vineyards.

Q: Are there any trails suitable for families with children in Simancas?
A: Yes, Simancas offers several easy routes that are suitable for families. The gentle terrain along the riverbanks and through some of the pine forests provides a pleasant experience for all ages. Look for routes marked as 'easy' in the komoot guide.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Simancas?
A: Many trails in Simancas are dog-friendly, especially those through natural areas like the pine forests and along the river paths. It's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash and ensure you clean up after them to preserve the natural environment for everyone.

Q: Are there viewpoints along the hiking trails in Simancas?
A: Yes, some routes offer scenic viewpoints. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10462858" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Simancas – View of the Cortados Cliffs loop from Simancas&lt;/a&gt; provides views of the Cortados Cliffs, offering picturesque vistas of the surrounding landscape.

Q: Is there parking available near the trailheads in Simancas?
A: Parking is generally available in and around Simancas, particularly near popular starting points for trails. Specific parking details can often be found on individual route pages on komoot or by checking local signage.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Simancas?
A: The spring and autumn months typically offer the most pleasant weather for hiking in Simancas, with milder temperatures and beautiful natural scenery. Summer can be warm, so early morning or late afternoon hikes are recommended, while winter can be cooler but still suitable for walking.

Q: Are there any long-distance or multi-day hiking options that pass through Simancas?
A: Yes, Simancas is part of significant long-distance routes. It lies on the extensive Senda del Duero, a multi-day route covering hundreds of kilometers. It is also a stop on the Camino de Madrid, specifically Stage 09, offering a moderate difficulty section for those seeking a Camino de Santiago experience.
tips: This impressive castle was built in the 15th century by the Admiral of Castile Don Fadrique Enríquez, later it passed into the hands of the monarchy.
It is located in the center of town, between the Hospital del Salvador and the Plaza del Mirador.

waypoints.name: Confluence of the Pisuerga and Duero Rivers
